channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Smog in Phoenix and Salt Lake City? The E.P.A. Is Blaming Asia.
item.description: The Trump administration says the cities shouldn’t be penalized for unhealthy air because pollution can blow in from abroad. Some experts say that’s preposterous.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Phoenix has long struggled with smog collecting in its bowl-shaped topography. The city failed to meet a federal air pollution standard in 2024.

item.title: Forest Service Research Labs Are Closing
item.description: A research lab in Washington State tracks ecological changes in a warming climate and provides scientific guidance for forest managers. It is one of 57 such facilities being shuttered.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler and John Stember
item.media:credit: John Stember for The New York Times

item.title: Global Deforestation Slows, W.R.I. Report Finds. But Wildfires Are Taking a Toll.
item.description: In 2025, the world razed less forest than any other year in the last decade. The bad news: global warming is making wildfires more frequent and intense.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Harry Stevens

item.title: How to Build a Better Kind of Nuclear Power? This Side Hustle Might Help.
item.description: Zap Energy says its ultimate goal is safe, clean energy from fusion. To help get there, it’s starting to build fission reactors.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Benj Conway, Zap’s president and co-founder.

item.title: FEMA Is Reversing Job Cuts Made Under Kristi Noem
item.description: The agency said staff members who had been let go or placed on administrative leave were now needed to prepare for hurricanes and the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: Former Homeland Security Kristi Noem during a visit to FEMA headquarters in Washington in January.

item.title: The World Met to Talk Climate Change. The U.S. Wasn’t Invited.
item.description: Dozens of countries met this week to discuss how to end the world’s dependence on fossil fuels, a goal that the Trump administration doesn’t share.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Delegates gathered for a meeting on transitioning away from fossil fuels in Santa Marta, Colombia, on Wednesday.

item.title: Almost All of Europe Was Abnormally Hot in 2025, Report Finds
item.description: The continent, which is warming faster than the global average, saw wildfires, floods and a sub-Arctic heat wave, the European State of the Climate report said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.category: European Center for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times
item.media:description: A scorching day in Seville, Spain, last July.

item.title: Loss of Emirates Further Weakens OPEC’s Influence
item.description: The exit of the United Arab Emirates is the most significant in a series of departures from the oil cartel in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil storage in Big Spring, Texas. OPEC is less powerful than it once was, in no small part because of the rise of U.S. oil production.

item.title: The Murky Ethics of Swimming With Killer Whales
item.description: Only two places in the world allow tourists to enter the water with the ocean’s apex predator. But the safety of both species is a growing concern.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Meghan Dhaliwal
item.media:credit: Meghan Dhaliwal for The New York Times

item.title: He Defied the State to Fortify His Mansion. Now He Wants to Be Governor.
item.description: Rom Reddy has pushed to protect his beachfront mansion in South Carolina from what he calls “government overreach.” The fight, he said, inspired his political awakening.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Donaven Doughty for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rom Reddy said he was eager to shift the focus from his wall to his nascent campaign, which kicked off last month, much later than those of the other candidates.

item.title: The E.P.A.’s Lost Science
item.description: The agency’s prestigious research office is being dismantled by the Trump administration, a plan to dam the Bering Strait and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Cornell Watson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bryan Hubbell, the former head of the air, climate and energy program at the Environmental Protection Agency’s research office. “The state of science is struggling,” he said.

item.title: The World Needs Natural Gas Now, but the U.S. Is Exporting All It Can
item.description: Even the largest global supplier of liquefied natural gas can’t make up for the shortfall since the war in Iran cut off an important source.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The United States is building more export terminals like this one in Cameron, La., but they will take months or years to complete.

item.title: Trump Administration Will Pay to Cancel More Wind Farms
item.description: In exchange, the companies will invest in oil and gas projects, echoing an earlier deal with the French energy giant TotalEnergies.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:description: President Trump has disparaged wind power for decades, claiming falsely that offshore wind turbines do not work and that they are killing whales.

item.title: How the Trump Administration Ended Independent Science at E.P.A.
item.description: The agency’s prestigious research office spent decades doing scientific work insulated from political pressure. Now it’s being dismantled.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Cornell Watson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bryan Hubbell, former head of the air, climate and energy program at the Environmental Protection Agency’s research office. “I knew I wasn’t going to be able to keep doing the work.”

item.title: The Rise of the High-Range, Less Expensive E.V.
item.description: Even as the electric vehicle market has slumped, there are more long-range E.V.s under $40,000 than ever before.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Paris

item.title: 60 Countries to Meet on Phasing Out Fossil Fuels but Are Excluding the U.S.
item.description: The Trump administration was not invited to the gathering in Santa Marta, Colombia. A White House spokeswoman called the green transition “destructive.”
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Dutch and Colombian environment officials at last year’s U.N. climate summit in Belém, Brazil, where this week’s First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels in Santa Marta was announced.

item.title: New York Keeps Getting Hotter. Utilities Can Still Cut Off the Power.
item.description: A new statewide policy detailing when utilities can stop service for unpaid bills during heat waves has resulted in weaker rules for New York City.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:description: Global warming is accelerating, and future heat waves are projected to be longer. New York State rules restrict electricity terminations only on the hottest days.

item.title: Supreme Court to Hear Arguments in Landmark Roundup Weedkiller Case
item.description: A victory for the manufacturer, Bayer, could end thousands of lawsuits against the company claiming that the herbicide causes cancer.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court in Washington. The Trump administration has formally backed Bayer in the case.

item.title: 40 Years After the Meltdown, War Layers Another Disaster on Chernobyl
item.description: Ideas have been floated for how the contaminated zone could bring economic benefits to Ukraine. But for the foreseeable future, it will be an army-controlled security belt.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer, Evelina Riabenko and Brendan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times

item.title: Thousands of EV Car Leases Are Ending Soon, Giving Buyers a More Affordable Option
item.description: The leases on hundreds of thousands of battery-powered cars and trucks will end in the next three years, and many will end up on used-car lots.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:description: Analysts say lower prices of used electric cars and the surging cost of gasoline will encourage more people to consider models that they may have found too expensive earlier.

item.title: A New Idea to Save the AMOC? Dam the Bering Strait.
item.description: Blocking the narrow waterway between Russia and Alaska could help stabilize a vulnerable system of ocean currents, scientists found in a study.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: A satellite view of the Bering Strait, which separates northeastern Russia and Alaska, in summer.

item.title: How the Supreme Court’s Shadow Docket Upended Climate Policy
item.description: The Times unearthed memos that signaled a major shift in the court’s operations, in a decision that critics say was rushed and flawed.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court has used a “shadow docket” to grant President Trump more than 20 victories on issues like immigration and employee firings.

item.title: A New Bureau Will Oversee Both Offshore Drilling and Seabed Mining
item.description: The new federal office will undo a change made after the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil disaster. Critics say it could reduce environmental oversight.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Drilling platforms off California in 2021. The Trump administration said the move would speed approvals of offshore energy and mining projects.

item.title: China Publishes Maps Detailing Minerals on the Ocean Floor
item.description: The new deep-sea atlas underscores Beijing’s interest in ocean mining, its military ambitions and its claims to disputed waters.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: A Chinese submersible in the South China Sea in 2017 during exploration for seabed mineral resources.

item.title: Sewage Spill Is Over, But Contamination Lingers In Potomac
item.description: Though river monitoring shows bacteria levels have declined, scientists and environmentalists said a full recovery isn’t yet assured.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.category: District of Columbia Water and Sewer Authority (DC Water)
item.media:credit: Michael Noble Jr. for The New York Times

item.title: Why Diesel Has Become a Much Bigger Economic Problem Than Gasoline
item.description: The war in Iran has disrupted supplies of diesel, used to power trucks and heavy equipment, much more than gasoline, which is primarily used in passenger cars.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Adam Amengual for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Port of Los Angeles. Large semi trucks and some ships run on diesel fuel.

item.title: Wildfires in Georgia Destroy Homes and Set Off Evacuations
item.description: The fires are also causing poor air quality, expected to last into at least Thursday, for a corner of the Southeast.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff, Aimee Ortiz and Hannah Ziegler

item.title: On Earth Day, House Cancels Vote to Narrow Endangered Species Protections
item.description: House leaders abruptly canceled a vote on the measure when support started to look shaky.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Catrin Einhorn
item.media:description: A grizzly bear in Yellowstone National Park in Wyoming last summer. Grizzlies are one of several species whose populations have rebounded in some areas in the past several decades.

item.title: Does Clothing Recycling Really Work?
item.description: Collection services offer convenience, but most garments are shredded into low-grade stuffing or sent abroad to an uncertain fate.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Cernansky
item.media:credit: Mikayla Whitmore for The New York Times
item.media:description: A sorting facility for Supercircle in North Las Vegas this month. The start-up aims to help the fashion industry use discarded textiles to make new clothes.

item.title: Olafur Eliasson Uses Art and Sound to Raise Climate Awareness in Utah
item.description: The Danish Icelandic artist reflects on his childhood and how it influenced and empowered his art.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: The Vienna Climate Biennale Contrasts Chaos with Hope
item.description: In a world marked by ecological, social and political crises, the art festival aims to get people talking, thinking and ready to imagine a better world.
item.dc:creator: Keridwen Cornelius

item.title: An Installation in Nature has Climate Lessons for Humans
item.description: With lichen, mechanical artwork and a barrel of snowflakes, “Climate Clock” will wind along a trail through the forests across Oulu, Finland.
item.dc:creator: David Belcher

item.title: Judge Halts Trump Actions That Have Slowed Renewable Energy
item.description: The Interior Department had imposed restrictions on wind and solar projects across the country, prompting developers to sue.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: David Robert Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: The case centers on actions by the Interior Department that slowed or restricted federal approvals for wind and solar projects on both public and private lands.

item.title: For Earth Day, a Few Signs of Hope for Our Planet
item.description: In a year of grim climate and environment news, we’ve compiled several hopeful signs about our planet’s future.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: Supreme Court of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: The view of home from the other side of the moon.

item.title: Rainforests Can Bounce Back Much Faster Than Thought, Researchers Say
item.description: Scientists long believed it would take more than a century for animals to return to deforested land. New research shows that’s not always the case.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey

item.title: E.P.A., Maryland Sue D.C. Utility Over Potomac River Sewage
item.description: In separate lawsuits, environmental regulators said D.C. Water failed to prevent and contain a massive spill of raw sewage upstream of Washington.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.category: District of Columbia Water and Sewer Authority (DC Water)
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sewage-contaminated water flowing into a creek inside Chesapeake and Ohio Canal National Historical Park near Cabin John, Md., last month.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz May Not Return to Normal, Whether It’s Open or Closed
item.description: The energy industry is planning for a future where the choke point on Iran’s southern coast is a lot less important.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels off Musandam, Oman, on Monday. Iran will not forget how easy it is to strangle shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: What’s in a Name? For These Snails, Legal Protection
item.description: Scientists are debating the classification of threatened mollusks that an Indigenous community relies on for their way of life.
item.dc:creator: Zoë Lescaze and María José Zavala Balcázar

item.title: A Rocky, Snow-Barren Ski Season Concludes in Colorado
item.description: Faced with a devastating snow drought and record-high March temperatures, some resorts closed early. But at Aspen Mountain, die-hards celebrated the last day of the season on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri and Will Sardinsky

item.title: Environmental Groups Sue to Stop BP Kaskida Drilling Plan
item.description: Opponents of the project, known as Kaskida, say an accident could be even worse than the Deepwater Horizon spill. The company says it’s learned from the past.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: The Deepwater Horizon oil rig, operated by BP, burning in the Gulf of Mexico in 2010.

item.title: These Salmon Got High on Cocaine. That Wasn’t the Craziest Part.
item.description: Scientists in Sweden made an unexpected discovery when they exposed the fish to the illegal drug as well as another substance.
item.dc:creator: Annie Roth

item.title: Inside the Supreme Court’s Risky New Way of Doing Business
item.description: Secret memos obtained by The New York Times illuminate the origins of the court’s now-routine “shadow docket” rulings on presidential power.
item.dc:creator: Jodi Kantor and Adam Liptak

item.title: Key Excerpts From the Supreme Court’s Secret Memos
item.description: The New York Times obtained a trove of documents illuminating the inner workings of the court as it embraced a secretive track for making major decisions.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak and Jodi Kantor

item.title: Western States Need Water. San Diego Has Extra. Will They Make a Deal?
item.description: San Diego County is shopping a surplus of desalinated seawater to Western states that are facing increasingly urgent drought and short supplies.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: A view of San Diego and the Pacific Ocean from Cabrillo National Monument.

item.title: Amit Forlit, Accused of Hacking Climate Activists, Appears in Court
item.description: Amit Forlit is accused of running a hacking operation that aimed to thwart climate litigation at the behest of a lobbying firm working for an oil giant.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal District Court in New York City. Amit Forlit has been indicted on charges of conspiracy to commit computer hacking, conspiracy to commit wire fraud and wire fraud.

item.title: Japan’s Cherry Blossom Database, 1,200 Years Old, Has a New Keeper
item.description: The remarkable catalog of dates is one of the longest-running records of climate change. Its creator died, setting off a search for a successor.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:description: Cherry blossoms at Rokkakudo Temple in Kyoto, Japan, recently.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Oil Companies in Louisiana Coastal Lawsuits
item.description: The companies had asked the justices to clear the way to move environmental lawsuits out of state courts, to friendlier federal venues.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Bryan Tarnowski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wetlands along the Mississippi River as it nears the Gulf of Mexico in southeast Louisiana.

item.title: How Can I Protect My Car When the Weather Is Scorching?
item.description: Extreme heat is extremely hard on your vehicle, but a few simple precautions can help a lot.
item.dc:creator: Susan Shain
